title: Missing CSRF protection on state-changing form
summary: A form that submits state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE) was observed without a CSRF token or equivalent anti-forgery mechanism. Without CSRF protection, an attacker can craft a malicious page that silently submits requests on behalf of an authenticated user, potentially changing account settings, transferring funds, or modifying data.
why_it_matters: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) exploits the trust a web application places in the user's browser. Because browsers automatically attach cookies (including session cookies) to every request to a given origin, an attacker-controlled page can trigger state-changing actions without the user's knowledge. CSRF consistently appears in the OWASP Top 10 (A01:2021 Broken Access Control) and has been used in real-world attacks against banking, email, and social media platforms.
how_to_fix:
Implement one or more of the following CSRF defense mechanisms:

1. **Synchronizer Token Pattern** — Generate a unique, unpredictable token per session (or per request). Embed the token in a hidden form field and validate it server-side on every state-changing request.
2. **Double-Submit Cookie** — Set a random value in a cookie and require the same value to be submitted as a request parameter or custom header. The server verifies that both values match.
3. **Framework-native CSRF protection** — Most modern frameworks include built-in CSRF support:
   - Spring Security: enable CsrfFilter (enabled by default), use `<input type="hidden" name="${_csrf.parameterName}" value="${_csrf.token}"/>`
   - Django: use `{% csrf_token %}` template tag and ensure CsrfViewMiddleware is active
   - Express: use the `csurf` middleware (or `csrf-csrf` for double-submit cookie pattern)
   - Rails: `protect_from_forgery with: :exception` (enabled by default)
4. **SameSite cookies** — Set `SameSite=Strict` or `SameSite=Lax` on session cookies as a defense-in-depth measure. Note: SameSite alone is not a complete CSRF defense on older browsers.
5. **Custom request headers** — For AJAX-only endpoints, require a custom header (e.g., `X-Requested-With`) that cannot be sent cross-origin without CORS approval.
unsafe_example:
<!-- UNSAFE: form submits a state-changing POST without any CSRF token -->
<form action="/account/settings" method="POST">
  <input type="text" name="email" value="user@example.com">
  <button type="submit">Update Email</button>
</form>
safe_example:
<!-- SAFE: form includes a hidden CSRF token validated server-side -->
<form action="/account/settings" method="POST">
  <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="a1b2c3d4e5f6...">
  <input type="text" name="email" value="user@example.com">
  <button type="submit">Update Email</button>
</form>

<!-- Server-side validation (Express + csurf example) -->
// const csrf = require('csurf');
// app.use(csrf({ cookie: true }));
// In route handler: if token is missing or invalid, csurf returns 403 automatically.
developer_notes: If your application is a single-page app (SPA) that communicates via JSON APIs, you can use the custom header pattern (e.g., X-CSRF-Token) since cross-origin requests cannot set custom headers without CORS preflight approval. For server-rendered forms, the synchronizer token pattern remains the most robust approach. Always verify CSRF tokens on the server side — client-side checks alone provide no protection. When migrating a legacy application, audit all POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints to ensure none are unprotected.
verification_checklist: Every state-changing form includes a CSRF token in a hidden field or custom header
The server validates the CSRF token on every POST, PUT, and DELETE request and rejects requests with missing or invalid tokens
Session cookies have SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict set as a defense-in-depth measure
AJAX endpoints require a custom header (e.g., X-CSRF-Token) that is validated server-side
Framework-native CSRF middleware is enabled and has not been globally disabled

references.title: OWASP Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
OWASP Top 10 A01:2021 Broken Access Control
